title: Jayalalithaa's Challenge: Daring or Belligerence?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. All six source pages were checked for the two High Court rulings, judges' names, quoted English reports, monetary figures, deadlines, and closing date. The source confirms the English library-security headline says “if Order is not followed”; clearly visible OCR character errors and accidental word breaks were corrected only in the reading copy. This chapter's source-verified identity is 3984; its former OCR-derived number 3954 is not carried into the translation.
paragraphs: I believe all of you will remember that, while replying to the demand for grants for the Police Department in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa issued me a challenge.

Referring to my statement about the security of the Madras High Court and an observation made by its Chief Justice, Jayalalithaa challenged me to implead myself in that case. After compiling everything that had happened in the courts during the DMK Government, she went on to say in the Assembly:

> “Do Karunanidhi and the DMK believe that the High Court must be protected by a Central security force and that the State police cannot provide proper security? Are they prepared to implead themselves and file a petition in this case? I issue this as a challenge to Karunanidhi.”

My statement of 19 September 2015 did not make any unsupported claim. I cited a front-page report in the English daily *The Hindu* dated 19 September under the headline, “No Confidence in State Police, observes C.J.” It reported:
> “Chief Justice S.K. Kaul on Friday made an oral observation in the open Court that he has lost confidence in State Police.”
In other words, the report said that Chief Justice S.K. Kaul had openly told the Court on Friday that he had lost confidence in the State police.

When counsel for the Tamil Nadu Government argued that Central police protection was unnecessary in the courts and protection by the Tamil Nadu police was adequate, the Chief Justice intervened and asked: “What is wrong with providing Central security, as at airports and ports? Despite repeated orders, protests and demonstrations are conducted on the court premises. Therefore, there is no confidence in the Tamil Nadu police.”

If the Chief Justice expressed such a view, should the Chief Minister, who is responsible for the Police Department, give the people an explanation or not? When the Chief Justice of the High Court himself held this view, does it not establish that the Opposition parties' views about the Jayalalithaa Government's police were entirely true? I cited this and asked only what answer the Government had to the Chief Justice's observation reported in *The Hindu*.

In response, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa told the Assembly: “Karunanidhi says that the Madras High Court has no security and that the Tamil Nadu police have failed to provide protection. He therefore says the Central Industrial Security Force must be deployed.”
But a report in *The Hindu* itself on 15 September 2015 said:

> “Pulling up the State Government and the Police for failing to prevent protesters from entering its premises on Monday, the Madras High Court has called upon the authorities to take immediate steps to declare its premises a ‘High Security Zone’ and provide security cover by the Central Industrial Security Force (CISF).”
In my statement, I said:

> “Every view I expressed was one reported in the newspapers as having been stated by the judges. If Jayalalithaa's challenge is that I must implead myself and file a petition in Court merely for saying this, I am ready! If the Court accepts that the views I expressed were reported in the newspapers as having been stated by the judges, is Chief Minister Jayalalithaa prepared to accept that and apologise?”
As usual, the Chief Minister offered no answer whatsoever to this question.

The case concerning whether the Madras High Court complex should be protected by a Central force or by the State Government came before the High Court's First Bench on 30 October 2015. Chief Justice Sanjay Kishan Kaul and Justice T.S. Sivagnanam delivered their judgment, which was reported in the newspapers. The English daily *The Times of India* headlined its report:
> **High Court orders CISF cover on its premises** > > **Slams Govt., Wants Forces by Nov. 16**
The report said:

> “A new security system provided by the CISF should be operational at the Madras High Court by November 16, the first bench of the court has ordered, flaying the State Government's shifting stands on bringing the court under central security blanket. The Court asked the Tamil Nadu Government to deposit Rs. 16.6 crore, as demanded by the Centre for deploying CISF, within one week. > > “An irate first bench of Chief Justice Sanjay Kishan Kaul and Justice T.S. Sivagnanam said: ‘We note with a deep sense of regret that games are being played in respect of a sensitive issue like the security of the court. We express our deep anguish as this issue is being unnecessarily made into more of an ego issue with political overtures rather than looking into the larger canvas of security requirements of the high court.’”

That is, it was necessary for an independent organisation to provide temporary security to the High Court. The Madras High Court was therefore to receive Central-force protection from 16 November, initially for the next six months. The Tamil Nadu Government was directed to deposit within one week the ₹16.6 crore sought by the Union Government.

The judges further said they wished to express their regret that an important matter such as court security was being treated as a game, and their anguish that, instead of viewing it as a question of High Court security, the issue had unnecessarily been turned into one driven by political motives.

News has appeared today that the Tamil Nadu Government, acting in great haste, intends to appeal even this Madras High Court judgment in the Supreme Court. In the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly on 22 September 2015, Jayalalithaa claimed that the Chief Justice had not spoken in that manner and issued me her challenge. Is it now categorically clear how much truth there was in her claim, or is it not?
That is the account of the High Court judgment delivered on 30 October 2015. On 29 October, the same High Court delivered another judgment.

At Kotturpuram in Chennai, during the previous DMK Government, a magnificent library without equal in South Asia was built to commemorate the centenary of Perarignar Anna. Through various developments over the past four years, and through judgments periodically delivered by the Court, Tamil Nadu knows well how badly it has been neglected and allowed to deteriorate since Jayalalithaa came to power.
Hearing the case, the First Bench of the Madras High Court appointed a two-member committee consisting of advocates P.T. Asha and M. Sundar and directed it to inspect the library and file a report.
When the case was heard in August, the High Court judges ordered that the library's deficiencies be rectified, that it be properly maintained, and that it be improved.

When the case came up in September, the Government filed a status report on the maintenance work. Accepting it, the judges ordered the Government to file a plan stating when the maintenance work would be completed.

The case again came before the judges on 29 October 2015. The Government filed a plan and sought more time. Senior advocates Wilson and Manonmaniam, appearing for the petitioner, said that despite the High Court's order, the Government had not implemented it.
Finally, the judges made stern observations:

> “Why has the Government not implemented the High Court's order? It has not done so despite repeated directions and extensions of time. Why this hesitation? We are granting one final opportunity. By 8 January next year, all facilities must be provided at the Anna Centenary Library in accordance with the High Court's order, and a report must be filed before the Court. If it is not filed, we may have to order the Tamil Nadu School Education Secretary to appear in person.”
The English report carried the headline: “Beef up Security at Library or face action” and added, “Will Summon Edu. Secretary if Order is not followed: H.C.”

In this case, although the honourable judges have warned this Government many times about the Anna Centenary Library, the AIADMK Government has repeatedly continued to ignore those judgments. The sole reason for all of it is that the library was built during the DMK Government!

As Chief Minister, Jayalalithaa bears the great responsibility of respecting the judgments and observations of the Madras High Court, taking proper governmental action upon them, and protecting and nurturing a good and harmonious relationship between the Government and the judiciary. Moreover, the fact that the Tamil Nadu Government has lately been subjected to so many contempt-of-court proceedings will certainly bring the Jayalalithaa Government into disrepute.

Instead of paying close attention to all this, removing the errors, and conducting the administration constructively, she challenged me in the Legislative Assembly for issuing an evidence-based statement intended to explain clearly to the public what was in the High Court's mind.
Was Jayalalithaa's challenge an act of daring, or an act of belligerence? Tamil Nadu understands very well!
With affection,
M.K.
2 November 2015
